docs/integration-guide.md:549:7. Run `client.send_to(...)` with a test payload.
docs/integration-guide.md:620:4. **Tell `Client` where the broker storage lives** so `wait_for_pairing` can poll it:
docs/integration-guide.md:632:   path = client.create_pairing_qr(
docs/integration-guide.md:641:   # subsequent `send_to(...)` calls just work.
docs/integration-guide.md:642:   pairing = client.wait_for_pairing(timeout_seconds=120)
docs/integration-guide.md:643:   # `client.pairing_key` is now set; no further `set_pairing` call needed.
docs/integration-guide.md:656:7. `Client.wait_for_pairing` in your sender's send loop picks up the new pairing tuple.
docs/integration-guide.md:668:| Broker returns 409 (replay with different values) | Fallback banner — the pairing key from your sender's `wait_for_pairing` will not match this device's, so the user must paste the device's values. |
docs/integration-guide.md:670:| Sender's `wait_for_pairing` times out | Sender's responsibility — typically loop again with a fresh QR. The device-side pairing is still real; user can paste manually. |
docs/integration-guide.md:680:3. **Pending-pairing registry at the broker.** Before any decrypt, the broker checks `storage.is_reserved(pairing_id)` and 404s unknown IDs. An attacker who knows the public bootstrap key can mint a cryptographically valid envelope for an arbitrary uuid4, but it can't reach the decrypt path unless the Client previously reserved that ID via `create_pairing_qr(sender_broker_url=...)`.
docs/integration-guide.md:686:- Use Redis or Postgres for `BrokerStorage`. The shipped `InMemoryBrokerStorage` is single-process only — fine for the trading-bot example where the Client and broker share one Python process, but **insufficient for multi-process deployments**. `Client.create_pairing_qr(sender_broker_url=...)` calls `storage.reserve(pairing_id)`; if the broker app runs in a different uvicorn worker, that call's effect won't be visible to the broker's `is_reserved()` check and every pairing will 404. Use a shared backing store that implements the `BrokerStorage` Protocol atomically (one `reserve` call visible to all workers).
docs/integration-guide.md:696:- **`410 plugin missing, revoked, or not owned by sender`** — `plugin_id` (the row UUID) is wrong, or you accidentally used `plugin_identifier`. The two are distinct (see §5 vs §6).
docs/integration-guide.md:698:- **`409 nonce already used`** — your code reused a nonce somehow; SDK generates fresh nonces per `send_to`, so this means you're calling `send_to` with cached envelope bytes. Rebuild.
docs/integration-guide.md:705:- **`HostPreviewValidationError: hostPreview.X is N UTF-8 bytes; max is M`** — caught at `client.send_to` time. Trim the offending field; see §2 for the caps. UTF-8 byte counts, not characters — emoji and accented characters cost more than one byte.
docs/integration-guide.md:706:- **`422 manifest validation failed`** (cards/plugins publish) — Pydantic rejected the manifest body. The detail field tells you which check failed. Most common: `template required when renderer == 'template'`, `card_key_path required when card_type == 'live'`, `action endpoint not allowed by declared endpoints`. Mirror this validation client-side via `validatePluginManifest` (TS SDK) to catch before the round-trip.
docs/integration-guide.md:716:- **Single-segment wildcard surprise in `declaredEndpoints`.** `https://example.com/api/*` matches `https://example.com/api/v1` but NOT `https://example.com/api/v1/users`. `*` is one segment, never multiple. Card shows *"endpoint not declared"*. See §3.1 for the full glob grammar.
docs/integration-guide.md:717:- **`card_key` doesn't match `card_key_path` resolved on the plaintext.** The manifest declares `card_key_path: "$.order_id"`; the device extracts that on decrypt and uses it as the merge key. You pass `card_key="order-456"` to `upsert_card`. If the plaintext's `$.order_id` doesn't equal `"order-456"`, the device treats each upsert as a separate row — your "live" card behaves like an event card. The server doesn't cross-check; it's your responsibility.
docs/integration-guide.md:718:- **Template/script field mismatches.** Setting `template: {...}` on a `renderer: "script"` manifest, or omitting `template` on `renderer: "template"`. SDK validator catches both. Same with `cardType: "live"` requiring `cardKeyPath` and `cardType: "event"` rejecting it. If the SDK validator misses a case you hit, file a Phase 4.5 doc-bug.
docs/integration-guide.md:724:Publish every plugin release as a new row under the same `plugin_identifier`. `plugin_identifier` is the stable name, while `plugin_row_id` is the exact version row returned by `client.publish_plugin(...)`.
docs/integration-guide.md:726:Version strings use semver-lite: `MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH` with an optional prerelease suffix. Each numeric component is capped at 6 digits, and every publish for the same `(sender_id, plugin_identifier)` must be strictly greater than all existing versions.
docs/integration-guide.md:730:The device verifies the downloaded bundle against the row's SHA-256 `bundle_hash` and caches verified bundle bytes by hash for the process lifetime. On app restart, the cache is empty and the device re-fetches the same historical row's `signed_bundle_url`. Keep old bundle URLs available for as long as you want old inbox messages to remain renderable.
docs/integration-guide.md:802:The envelope itself is the same V2 shape you'd hand to `send_to(...)` — sealed for each of the user's devices with their per-device HPKE keys, signed by your Ed25519 key over the canonical bytes (`docs/crypto-spec.md §11.8`). The push endpoint treats the envelope as opaque; the authenticity gate is the envelope signature the devices verify, not a separate POST-level signature. Server fans out to **every paired user under your sender** for `plugin_row_id`, and each device picks its own `recipient_envelope` to decrypt. Devices that have no matching `recipient_envelope` on the frame just ignore it.
docs/integration-guide.md:812:1. **Register a `liveInboundUrl`** in your plugin manifest at publish time. The TypeScript SDK manifest exposes this field; the Python `Client.publish_plugin(...)` does not yet expose `live_inbound_url`, so Python integrators must drive the raw `POST /v1/plugins/publish` body to set it until the helper grows the parameter.
docs/integration-guide.md:818:POST <liveInboundUrl>
docs/integration-guide.md:891:For persistent live cards (`card_type: "live"`) that change frequently — scoreboards, typing indicators, presence dots — use `client.patch_card(...)` instead of paying the per-tick full-card seal cost of `upsert_card`. The wire frame is opaque (server NEVER sees the field path or the new value); per-recipient HPKE-sealed JSON patches ride inside.
docs/integration-guide.md:895:Use `upsert_card` when:
docs/integration-guide.md:902:Use `patch_card` when:
docs/integration-guide.md:903:- The card exists (an `upsert_card` already landed) and only a small set of fields needs to change.
docs/integration-guide.md:907:If you can't guarantee contiguous publishing — e.g. multiple worker processes might race on the same `(card_id, base_seq)` — fall back to `upsert_card` per tick. The server rejects gaps and replays with `409`; a non-publisher process trying to patch usually loses that race.
docs/integration-guide.md:912:client.patch_card(
docs/integration-guide.md:955:**What you need to do as an integrator**: nothing. Your sender's `send_to / upsert_card / patch_card / live push` paths are unchanged. The notification adapter gates on prefs before posting; if it suppresses, the message still arrives in the inbox.

 succeeded in 320ms:
25-### What the host does for you
26-
27-Once a card is in a user's inbox, the host handles all of the following without you doing anything plugin-side:
28-
29-- **Native row rendering** from your `hostPreview` block (title / subtitle / summary) plus the server's arrival time (HH:mm for today, MMM d for older).
30-- **Unread indicator** + read sync across the user's devices. A row marks read when the user opens the detail view; the state syncs via an encrypted CAS state blob.
31-- **Left navigation drawer** with selectable views: **Unread** (only items the user hasn't opened), **All** (the default), **Archive** (items the user moved out of the main flow), and **Groups** — an expandable list of every paired sender that lets the user filter the inbox down to one sender at a time. The top-bar title reflects the active view.
32-- **Inbox search** (case-insensitive substring) over title, subtitle, summary, sender name, AND any tokens you supplied in `hostPreview.searchText`.
33-- **Archive** + **Delete** + **Multi-select** with bulk Archive / Delete. Archive/delete state is local and synced across devices; deleted messages are hidden from inbox AND archive. The server retains messages until their normal expiry.
34-- **Mute sync** — the user can mute your plugin globally (all devices) or locally (this device only) from the host-owned settings sheet. Muted plugins are hidden from the active inbox but remain accessible in the Senders tab for unmuting.
35-- **Bottom navigation** (Inbox / Senders / Settings) — the user is always one tap away from your card.
36-- **Revocation UX** — if you revoke a plugin version with a `compromised` reason, the host refuses to execute the bundle on the device and shows a security banner instead. See §5.5.
37-- **Live cards pin above events.** When mixed in the same inbox, live cards (`card_type: "live"`) sort above event cards (`card_type: "event"`), each subsorted by recency. The "Live" view in the drawer shows only live cards.
38-- **Per-device dismiss (server-side filter).** When the user dismisses a message on device A, `/v1/messages/{id}/dismiss` writes a `DeliveryStatus` row keyed on `(message_id, device_id=A)`. Subsequent calls to `/v1/messages/inbox` from device A LEFT JOIN `DeliveryStatus` for `device_id=A` and filter rows where `dismissed_at IS NOT NULL` out of A's feed. The dismiss SSE event still fans out to *all* of the user's devices, but the server-side filter is per-device — devices B, C, etc. receive the hint and refresh, but their own feeds keep the row until they dismiss it themselves. Dismiss is a per-device gesture by design — some users want a card visible elsewhere; a cross-device "dismiss everywhere" toggle is on the roadmap.
39-- **Real-time hints over SSE.** Foreground devices keep an authenticated Server-Sent Events stream open to `/v1/events`. The server pushes content-blind hints — `inbox.changed`, `state.changed`, `dismiss`, `card.upsert`, `card.delete` — and the client re-fetches over REST in response. The actual payloads still flow over the existing pull endpoints; SSE is the wakeup signal, not the data channel. Backgrounded devices fall back to FCM.
40-- **Live channel.** Plugins can open a long-lived authenticated WebSocket via `platform.live.connect(channel)` for sub-second push from the sender to the device + outbound `send` from the device. The host owns the WS lifecycle (heartbeat, reconnect, rate limit, revocation-driven close); the server is a routing pipe — frames carry your V2-encrypted envelopes opaquely. **Partner wiring is not yet usable** — the device-side `LiveBridge` ships with a placeholder JWT provider that throws `no_session`; the sender-push and webhook-forwarder paths work but the device-side `platform.live.connect` will fail until the host wiring closes. See §12.
41:- **Field-level live-card patches.** Persistent live cards can be updated one field at a time with `client.patch_card(card_id, patches=[(field, value)])` — no whole-card re-encryption per tick. The host applies the patch on top of the existing card payload atomically; failed/missing patches in the chain fall back to the last full upsert. See §13.
42-- **Guided lost-device recovery.** The Settings → Security → "I lost a device" flow walks the user through device revoke → master-key rotation → re-pair handoff as a single security-recovery wizard. See §11.
43-- **Per-plugin user preferences.** The user can mute your plugin, override the displayed label, set notification cadence (realtime / 15-min / hourly / daily digest), and configure quiet hours — all without any plugin-side code. The OS notification is gated; the inbox row still updates so the user sees the missed event on next open. See §14.
44-
45-You don't need to opt into any of these. Populate `hostPreview` and they all work.
46-
47-## 2. Protocol
48-
49-### Payload (your backend -> user device)
50-
51-Two parts: a reserved **`hostPreview`** block the platform renders natively as the inbox row, and the rest of the payload that your plugin's `render()` consumes for the detail view.
52-
53-```json
54-{
55-  "hostPreview": {
56-    "title": "Something happened",
57-    "subtitle": "Account A • 12 minutes ago",
--
260-## 5. Publishing the plugin (your backend, one time per version)

278-    version="1.0.0",

281-        sort_keys=True, separators=(",", ":")
282-    ).encode()).digest(),

297-
298-### 5.1 Native Template Renderer
299-
300-Set `renderer="template"` to ship a Compose-rendered native card instead of a JS bundle. The publish call looks like this:
301-
302-```python
303:response = client.publish_plugin(
304-    plugin_identifier="com.example.weather",
305-    version="1.0.0",
306-    manifest_hash=hashlib.sha256(b"manifest-placeholder").digest(),
307-    bundle_hash=hashlib.sha256(b"unused-for-template").digest(),
308-    bundle_signature=b"\x00" * 64,            # ignored for template
309-    signed_bundle_url="https://example.com/unused",  # ignored for template
310-    capabilities=[],
311-    endpoints=["https://example.com/api/*"],
312-    renderer="template",
313-    template={
314-        "layout": "standard_card",
315-        "fields": {
316-            "title":    {"path": "$.title"},
317-            "subtitle": {"path": "$.subtitle"},
318-            "body":     {"path": "$.body"},
319-        },
--
337-**Actions.** Each action is a button on the card. When tapped, the host POSTs the **full decrypted payload as `application/json`** to `endpoint`. No `Authorization` header is attached — the host explicitly uses an unauthenticated HTTP client for action POSTs so the user's Syncler bearer token never reaches your plugin endpoint (security boundary). If you need request authentication, HMAC the body using a sender-specific secret your backend knows out of band, or rely on TLS + endpoint allowlisting alone.
338-
339-**Validation rules** the publish endpoint enforces (returns 422 otherwise):
340-
341-- `layout` is in the supported set.
342-- Required fields for the layout are present.
343-- No fields outside the layout's allowed set.
344-- Every `path` matches the JSONPath regex.
345-- Every `action.endpoint` matches at least one of your declared `endpoints` globs (the same glob syntax that gates `platform.network.fetch` in script renderers).
346-- Every `action.id` is unique within the template.
347-
348-### 5.2 Live Cards
349-
350-Set `card_type="live"` to ship a persistent, upsertable card. The plugin manifest must also provide `card_key_path` — a JSONPath into your decrypted payload that yields the stable identity for each card (e.g. `$.order_id`). This lets the inbox merge multiple upserts for the same logical card into one row.
351-
352-```python
353:response = client.publish_plugin(
354-    plugin_identifier="com.example.delivery",
355-    version="1.0.0",
356-    manifest_hash=hashlib.sha256(b"manifest").digest(),
357-    bundle_hash=hashlib.sha256(open("dist/plugin.bundle.js","rb").read()).digest(),
358-    bundle_signature=bytes.fromhex(manifest["signature"]),
359-    signed_bundle_url="https://example.com/plugin.bundle.js",
360-    capabilities=["network"],
361-    endpoints=["https://example.com/api/*"],
362-    card_type="live",
363-    card_key_path="$.order_id",
364-)
365-```
366-
367-Live cards work with **either** renderer. The most common combo is `renderer="template"` + `card_type="live"` (delivery progress card with no JS).
368-
369-#### Upserting a live card
--
390-    "title": "Pizza Delivery",
391-    "subtitle": "Order #456 • ETA 8 minutes",
392-    "body": "Driver: Sam. Truck: 7F-12.",
393-}).encode("utf-8")
394-
395-# AAD the device will verify against on AES-GCM decrypt. `sequence_number`
396-# stays as a Python int — the canonical JSON emits an integer literal.
397-aad = assemble_live_card_aad(
398-    sender_id=client.sender_id,
399-    user_id=user_id,
400-    plugin_id=plugin_row_id,
401-    card_key="order-456",
402-    sequence_number=2,
403-    expires_at=expires_at,
404-)
405-
406:# V2 (current): client.upsert_card takes the plaintext payload + card_type
407-# directly — the SDK handles per-device V2 envelope sealing (HPKE per
408-# recipient device + AES-GCM payload, AAD bound to envelope_kind +
409-# card_type + sequence_number per docs/crypto-spec.md §11). You don't
410-# touch encrypt_payload / nonce yourself anymore.
411:client.upsert_card(
412-    user_id=user_id,
413-    plugin_id=plugin_row_id,
414-    card_key="order-456",
415-    card_type="standard_card",
416-    payload={
417-        "hostPreview": {"title": "Order #456", "summary": "Shipped"},
418-        "order_id": "order-456",
419-        "status": "shipped",
420-    },
421-    sequence_number=2,
422-    expires_at=expires_at,  # datetime, server caps at now + 48h
423-)
424-```
425-
426-**`card_key` must equal `card_key_path` resolved on the plaintext.** The manifest's `card_key_path` (declared at publish time, e.g. `"$.order_id"`) is the path the host extracts on decrypt to use as the merge key for this card. The `card_key` you pass to `upsert_card` is what the server stores. If the two disagree, the host's per-card merge breaks and your "live" card behaves like a series of event cards. The server doesn't cross-check the two — enforcement is your responsibility. (V1 validates `card_key_path` only as `startswith("$")`; a future phase will tighten this to the full `$.field(.subfield)*` JSONPath grammar used by template fields.)
427-
--
466-
467-| Reason | Use when | Host UX |
468-|---|---|---|
469-| `superseded` | A newer version replaces this row. | Shows a subdued "newer version available" banner in detail; the historical bundle can still render. |
470-| `compromised` | The bundle or signing key is unsafe. | Refuses to execute the bundle and shows a security banner with the decrypted payload fallback. |
471-| `sender_disabled` | The sender/service is intentionally disabled. | Shows a neutral "sender unavailable" banner in detail; the historical bundle can still render. |
472-| `unspecified` | You are migrating legacy tooling and cannot classify the revoke. | Shows a generic revoked banner; avoid using this for new revokes. |
473-
474-The revoke request is signed by the SDK. The `reason` value is included in the signed envelope, so an intermediary cannot strip `compromised` down to a softer classification. Re-revoking a row keeps the original `revoked_at`; a higher-severity reason can promote the row, while downgrades are ignored.
475-

536-
537-## 8. Testing it end-to-end
538-
539-> **Starting a new plugin from scratch?** Run `npm create @syncler/plugin <name>` first — it scaffolds `src/plugin.ts`, `manifest.json`, `build.sh`, `package.json`, `README.md`, and `.gitignore` for you, validated against the SDK rules. Pair it with the `examples/trading-bot/` directory for a complete sender + plugin round-trip you can copy from.
540-
541-1. Install the Syncler Android app (`./gradlew :app:installDebug`).
542-2. Sign up + log in. The app auto-enrolls this device with the server.
543-3. From your backend, run a script that registers your sender and prints a pairing QR (see SDK README).
544-4. Tap **Pair sender** -> **Scan QR** in the Syncler app. Confirm the fingerprint matches what your script prints.
545-5. **Pairing handoff.** Two paths depending on how your sender is set up:
546:   - **V1.5 automated (default going forward):** if your sender has registered a bootstrap key and supplied `sender_broker_url` at `create_pairing_qr(...)` time, the app POSTs an encrypted bootstrap envelope (containing `user_id` + `pairing_key`, sealed with the sender's X25519 bootstrap public key) to your broker after fingerprint confirmation; your sender's `Client.wait_for_pairing(...)` returns the decrypted pair. No copy step. See §8.5 for the broker setup.
547-   - **V1 manual:** the app shows your `user_id` and a `pairing_key_hex`. Copy both and feed them into your backend's `client.set_pairing(user_id, pairing_key=bytes.fromhex(...))` so it can encrypt for you. This is also the fallback when the broker POST fails in the automated path.
548-6. Publish your plugin (`client.publish_plugin(...)`) — save the `plugin_row_id`.
549-7. Run `client.send_to(...)` with a test payload.
550-8. The server pushes an `inbox.changed` event over the SSE stream the open app is subscribed to (foreground); the phone refreshes its inbox, decrypts the message, fetches your bundle (or applies your template manifest), and shows the native row within ~1s. If the app is backgrounded, FCM wakes it and the inbox refresh happens on the next foreground.
551-9. Tap the row to open the plugin-rendered detail view, then tap your action button.
552-10. Confirm your `/api/action` endpoint received the POST.
553-
554-A complete `bot.py` + `plugin/` pair you can run as-is lives at `examples/trading-bot/` — `python bot.py register` → `pair` → `publish-plugin` → `ack-server` + `loop` produces a real card with a working Acknowledge round-trip. The example uses the automated V1.5 pairing flow with an in-process broker thread; `set-pairing` is preserved as a V1 manual fallback when the broker can't be reached.
555-
556-## 8.5 Automated pairing (V1.5)
557-
558-V1 pairing makes the user copy `user_id` + `pairing_key_hex` by hand into your sender's CLI. V1.5 replaces step 5 above with an encrypted POST: after the user confirms the fingerprint, the app silently delivers the pairing key to your sender's broker. The user never sees a hex blob.
559-
560-The protocol underneath (HPKE-style envelope, X25519 ECDH, AES-GCM with AAD-binding) is in `docs/crypto-spec.md §9`. This section is the integration walkthrough.
561-
562-### Why automated
--
604-
605-   app = make_app(
606-       bootstrap_private_key=priv,
607-       bootstrap_public_key_raw=pub_raw,
608-       sender_broker_url="https://sender.example.com/syncler/bootstrap",
609-       storage=storage,
610-   )
611-   ```
612-
613-   ```sh
614-   uvicorn broker_app:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8443 \
615-       --ssl-keyfile=key.pem --ssl-certfile=cert.pem
616-   ```
617-
618-   Your reverse proxy maps `https://sender.example.com/syncler/bootstrap` → this app.
619-
620:4. **Tell `Client` where the broker storage lives** so `wait_for_pairing` can poll it:
621-
622-   ```python
623-   client = Client(
624-       sender_name="Example",
625-       private_key_path="sender.pem",
626-       broker_storage=storage,            # NEW — same storage the broker app writes to
627-   )
628-   # The sender's X25519 bootstrap keypair lives outside Client (the
629-   # broker app holds it). The Client only needs the storage handle
630-   # to learn when a pairing has completed.
631-
632:   path = client.create_pairing_qr(
633-       ttl_seconds=300,
634-       out_path="pair.png",
635-       sender_broker_url="https://sender.example.com/syncler/bootstrap",  # NEW
636-   )
637-
638-   # Blocks until the broker writes (user_id, pairing_key) to storage.
639-   # On success the Pairing dataclass carries `pairing_id` + `user_id`;
640-   # the 32-byte pairing key is stored on `client.pairing_key` so
641-   # subsequent `send_to(...)` calls just work.
642:   pairing = client.wait_for_pairing(timeout_seconds=120)
643-   # `client.pairing_key` is now set; no further `set_pairing` call needed.
644-   ```
645-
646-### Android user flow
647-
648-When the device fetches the preview and sees all four bootstrap fields, the pairing screen uses the automated path:
649-
650-1. User scans the QR.
651-2. App fetches preview; verifies `bootstrap_key_signature` against the sender's Ed25519 pub key.
652-3. User confirms the fingerprint (unchanged).
653-4. App calls `/complete` — Syncler-side pairing is finalized.
654-5. App builds the bootstrap envelope and POSTs to `sender_broker_url`.
655-6. Broker decrypts, writes to its storage, returns 201.
656:7. `Client.wait_for_pairing` in your sender's send loop picks up the new pairing tuple.
657-
658-If step 5 fails (broker down, network glitch), the app **shows a fallback banner** with the same `user_id` + `pairing_key_hex` block as the V1 manual flow. The device-side pairing is already real (step 4 finalized it); only the sender catch-up needs the manual paste.
659-
660-### Failure modes
661-
662-| What | App behavior |
663-|---|---|
664-| `bootstrap_key_signature` doesn't verify | **Hard refusal.** Pairing NOT finalized. Treated as substitution-attack indicator — user does not get a fallback. |
665-| Preview has only some bootstrap fields | **Hard refusal**, same reason. Sender MUST register all four atomically. |
666-| Broker returns 401 (decrypt failed) | Fallback banner (manual paste). |
667-| Broker returns 404 (pairing slot not found) | Fallback banner. Usually a multi-process miswire: the Client's `reserve()` call landed in a different process / worker than the broker's `is_reserved()` check. Use a shared `BrokerStorage` backend (Redis, Postgres) so reservations propagate. |
668:| Broker returns 409 (replay with different values) | Fallback banner — the pairing key from your sender's `wait_for_pairing` will not match this device's, so the user must paste the device's values. |
669-| Broker returns 5xx or times out | Retried up to 3 attempts (250ms / 750ms backoff). If still failing, fallback banner. |
670:| Sender's `wait_for_pairing` times out | Sender's responsibility — typically loop again with a fresh QR. The device-side pairing is still real; user can paste manually. |
671-
672-### Security boundaries
673-
674-Four guards make this safe:
675-
676-1. **Bootstrap key signature.** The sender's X25519 bootstrap public key is signed by its long-term Ed25519 signing key (which the user confirms via fingerprint). The Syncler server cannot substitute its own X25519 key — the signature would fail to verify against the Ed25519 pub key the user just confirmed.
677-
678-2. **AAD-binding on `sender_broker_url`.** Even if the Syncler server could change which URL the device POSTs to, the broker reconstructs AAD using its OWN configured `sender_broker_url`. An envelope crafted for a different URL fails AEAD tag verification.
679-
680:3. **Pending-pairing registry at the broker.** Before any decrypt, the broker checks `storage.is_reserved(pairing_id)` and 404s unknown IDs. An attacker who knows the public bootstrap key can mint a cryptographically valid envelope for an arbitrary uuid4, but it can't reach the decrypt path unless the Client previously reserved that ID via `create_pairing_qr(sender_broker_url=...)`.
681-
682-4. **CAS replay guard at the broker.** A captured envelope replayed to the same broker with the same plaintext is idempotent (200). Replayed with different values gets a 409.
683-
684-### Production hardening
685-
686:- Use Redis or Postgres for `BrokerStorage`. The shipped `InMemoryBrokerStorage` is single-process only — fine for the trading-bot example where the Client and broker share one Python process, but **insufficient for multi-process deployments**. `Client.create_pairing_qr(sender_broker_url=...)` calls `storage.reserve(pairing_id)`; if the broker app runs in a different uvicorn worker, that call's effect won't be visible to the broker's `is_reserved()` check and every pairing will 404. Use a shared backing store that implements the `BrokerStorage` Protocol atomically (one `reserve` call visible to all workers).
687-- The `rate_limiter` hook on `make_app(...)` is **mandatory** in production. The pending-pairing registry blocks envelopes for un-issued `pairing_id`s, but once an attacker knows a reserved ID they can still spam decrypt attempts on it — the rate limiter is your CPU defense. Implement per-IP and per-`pairing_id` limits.
688-- Terminate TLS at your reverse proxy; the broker is HTTP-only inside the trust boundary.
689-- See `docs/crypto-spec.md §9.3` for the single-fixed-`sender_broker_url` design (per-pairing URLs are a V2 add).
690-
691-## 9. Common server errors
692-
693-The server returned a 4xx or 5xx; here's what to change.
694-
695-- **`401 invalid envelope signature`** — your sender's Ed25519 key doesn't match the one registered. Re-check `private_key_path`.
696-- **`410 plugin missing, revoked, or not owned by sender`** — `plugin_id` (the row UUID) is wrong, or you accidentally used `plugin_identifier`. The two are distinct (see §5 vs §6).
697-- **`410 recipient has no active devices`** — the user has no enrolled device (signed up but never opened the app, or revoked all devices). FCM availability is *not* required; messages are stored regardless and pulled via `/v1/messages/inbox`.
698-- **`409 nonce already used`** — your code reused a nonce somehow; SDK generates fresh nonces per `send_to`, so this means you're calling `send_to` with cached envelope bytes. Rebuild.
699-- **`429 rate limited`** — back off + retry per `Retry-After` header.
700-- **`410 plugin missing, revoked, not live-type, or not owned by sender`** (cards upsert) — the plugin row's `card_type` isn't `"live"`, or the row has a non-null `revoked_at`, or the publish row's `sender_id` doesn't match the signed envelope's sender. The error message is intentionally generic so a probing caller can't distinguish the cases (don't leak which plugin UUIDs exist).
701-- **`410 no active pairing`** (cards upsert) — the recipient user has not paired with your sender, or the pairing has been revoked. The user needs to re-scan your pairing QR.
702-- **`400 expires_at exceeds the 48h live-card cap`** (cards upsert) — your supplied `expires_at` is more than 48 hours from now. Live cards are capped server-side to prevent slot squatting. Send shorter and re-upsert to extend.
--
875-- `platform.live.connect(channelName)` opens (or reuses) the WS for this plugin row and starts the named channel. Idempotent — calling twice on the same channel just returns success.
876-- `platform.live.send(channel, envelopeBase64)` MUST be preceded by `connect`. Calling `send` on an un-connected channel returns `channel_not_open`.
877-- `platform.live.close(channel)` is idempotent. Closing one channel does NOT tear down the underlying WS for other channels.
878-- `platform.live.subscribe(channelName)` is a one-line alias for `connect` that emphasizes read-only intent; same wire path.
879-
880-**Inbound delivery on the TypeScript SDK is outbound-only today.** The host receives device-bound live frames and dispatches them through an `onLiveMessage` hook on the Android plugin runtime, but the TypeScript `BasePlugin` does not yet expose that hook (the `DispatchHook` union on the SDK side covers `onMessage / onAction / onDismiss / render` only). Two paths for now:
881-
882-- If your plugin only needs to PUSH from the device → sender, the existing `platform.live.send` + the server-to-sender webhook forwarder is the full path. Use the inbox / SSE path for sender → device.
883-- If you need device → device or full bidirectional, wait for the SDK ABI bump that wires the inbound hook through.
884-
885-### Operator note (production)
886-
887-The server's live fan-out is backed by a Redis transport in any multi-worker deployment (`LIVE_BACKPLANE=redis` + `REDIS_URL`). For single-worker dev the default `memory` backplane works without Redis. The integrator doesn't pick this; the operator does. Spec: `docs/live-backplane.md`.
888-
889-## 13. Field-level live-card patches
890-
891:For persistent live cards (`card_type: "live"`) that change frequently — scoreboards, typing indicators, presence dots — use `client.patch_card(...)` instead of paying the per-tick full-card seal cost of `upsert_card`. The wire frame is opaque (server NEVER sees the field path or the new value); per-recipient HPKE-sealed JSON patches ride inside.
892-
893-### When to use which
894-
895-Use `upsert_card` when:
896-- You're publishing the card for the first time.
897-- The card's identity (`card_key`, `card_type`) or schema changed.
898-- The `hostPreview` block changes (title / subtitle / summary).
899-- You need to extend the TTL beyond the current `expires_at`.
900-- You're recovering from a sequencing mistake — a new upsert resets the patch chain.
901-
902-Use `patch_card` when:
903-- The card exists (an `upsert_card` already landed) and only a small set of fields needs to change.
904-- Your update rate is high enough that re-sealing the whole payload per tick hurts.
905-- You can maintain **contiguous `patch_seq`** numbering (one publisher per card; no gaps).
906-
907-If you can't guarantee contiguous publishing — e.g. multiple worker processes might race on the same `(card_id, base_seq)` — fall back to `upsert_card` per tick. The server rejects gaps and replays with `409`; a non-publisher process trying to patch usually loses that race.
908-
909-### Sender side
910-
911-```python
912:client.patch_card(
913-    user_id="...",
914-    plugin_id="...",
915-    card_id="...",         # the live card's row UUID, returned by the prior upsert
916-    base_seq=42,           # must equal the card's current sequence_number
917-    patch_seq=7,           # contiguous (= last_patch_seq + 1)
918-    patches=[
919-        ("home_score", "42"),
920-        ("away_score", "17"),
921-    ],
922-    field_paths={          # name → JSONPath mapping; SDK never sees raw paths in your code
923-        "home_score": "$.home_score",
924-        "away_score": "$.away_score",
925-    },
926-)
927-```
928-

docs/crypto-spec.md-223-
docs/crypto-spec.md-224-Live cards are persistent, upsertable units identified by `(sender_id, user_id, card_key)`. The encrypted payload uses the same AES-256-GCM primitive as messages, but with a richer AAD that binds the card-specific metadata. Upsert and delete each have their own Ed25519 envelope distinct from the message envelope.
docs/crypto-spec.md-225-
docs/crypto-spec.md-226-### 8.1 Live Card AAD
docs/crypto-spec.md-227-
docs/crypto-spec.md-228-Canonical JSON, UTF-8, sorted keys, compact separators (same encoder as §4):
docs/crypto-spec.md-229-

docs/crypto-spec.md-231-{
docs/crypto-spec.md-232-  "card_key": "...",

docs/crypto-spec.md-234-  "expires_at": "<ISO8601 UTC with Z suffix>",
docs/crypto-spec.md-235-  "plugin_id": "...",
docs/crypto-spec.md-236-  "sender_id": "...",

docs/crypto-spec.md-240-```
docs/crypto-spec.md-241-
docs/crypto-spec.md:242:`card_type` is always the string `"live"` (the field exists so the AAD shape can extend cleanly when future card types land — e.g. ephemeral or scheduled cards). `sequence_number` is emitted as a JSON integer literal (no quotes); senders MUST NOT stringify it. `expires_at` MUST use the `Z` UTC suffix (not `+00:00`) so SDK and server agree on the canonical bytes.
docs/crypto-spec.md-243-
docs/crypto-spec.md-244-The AAD binds the ciphertext to a specific (sender, user, plugin, card, sequence, expiry) tuple. A replay of an older sequence or a substitution of one card's ciphertext under another's metadata will fail AES-GCM tag verification on decrypt.

docs/crypto-spec.md-951-The wire format splits `enc` and `ciphertext` into separate base64 fields (`hpke_kem_output` + `hpke_ciphertext`) for schema-level validation cleanliness. Implementations re-concatenate them before calling `Suite.decrypt`. The 32/48/80 byte split is derived from the suite constants; implementations MUST NOT hardcode the numbers outside KEM-specific tests — a future suite swap fails closed.
docs/crypto-spec.md-952-
docs/crypto-spec.md-953-PyCA's single-shot HPKE only accepts `info`; it does not expose RFC 9180's separate `aad`. To match across platforms, Phase 9 uses `aad = empty` and folds ALL per-recipient authenticated context into `info`. Tink callers pass `aad = byte[0]` and the same canonical `info` bytes.
docs/crypto-spec.md-954-
docs/crypto-spec.md-955-### 11.3 Per-Recipient HPKE Info (canonical JSON)
docs/crypto-spec.md-956-
docs/crypto-spec.md-957-For each `recipient_envelope[i]` the sender builds a per-recipient `info` blob, sorted-keys canonical JSON, UTF-8, compact separators `(",", ":")`:
docs/crypto-spec.md-958-
docs/crypto-spec.md-959-```json
docs/crypto-spec.md-960-{
docs/crypto-spec.md:961:  "card_key": "...",            (live_card_upsert only)
docs/crypto-spec.md:962:  "card_type": "...",           (live_card_upsert only)
docs/crypto-spec.md-963-  "device_id": "<uuid>",
docs/crypto-spec.md:964:  "envelope_kind": "event" | "live_card_upsert",
docs/crypto-spec.md-965-  "expires_at": "<ISO8601 UTC>",
docs/crypto-spec.md-966-  "min_plugin_version": "...",
docs/crypto-spec.md-967-  "payload_ciphertext_sha256": "<lowercase hex>",
docs/crypto-spec.md-968-  "payload_nonce": "<base64 12-byte>",
docs/crypto-spec.md-969-  "plugin_id": "<uuid>",
docs/crypto-spec.md-970-  "protocol_version": 2,
docs/crypto-spec.md-971-  "sender_id": "<uuid>",
docs/crypto-spec.md:972:  "sequence_number": <int>,     (live_card_upsert only)
docs/crypto-spec.md-973-  "user_id": "<uuid>"
docs/crypto-spec.md-974-}
docs/crypto-spec.md-975-```
docs/crypto-spec.md-976-
docs/crypto-spec.md:977:Live-card upsert info adds `card_key`, `card_type`, `sequence_number`. The presence/absence of these keys is the canonical signal of the envelope kind — implementations MUST omit keys (not emit `null`) so kind A's info cannot collide with kind B's at the byte level.
docs/crypto-spec.md-978-
docs/crypto-spec.md-979-`payload_ciphertext_sha256` binds the CEK wrap to one payload: an attacker can't take recipient D's HPKE wrap and combine it with a different `payload_ciphertext` because HPKE seal failure-closed verifies info.
docs/crypto-spec.md-980-
docs/crypto-spec.md-981-`device_id` makes each recipient's info unique; one device's wrap cannot be replayed at another device even if the CEK is somehow recovered.

docs/crypto-spec.md-1085-**Verify order (Codex 127 implementation guardrail):** server / device MUST validate the Ed25519 signature BEFORE consulting any envelope field for routing, storage, or HPKE info reconstruction. The unsigned wire bytes are untrusted until the signature passes. Implementations that read `sender_id` from the envelope to look up the verification key MUST re-verify the signature with the *resolved* sender's known public key, never trust an inline pubkey.

sdk-plugin\tools\sign-bundle.ts-1-/**
sdk-plugin\tools\sign-bundle.ts-2- * Sign a plugin bundle for Syncler.
sdk-plugin\tools\sign-bundle.ts-3- *
sdk-plugin\tools\sign-bundle.ts:4: * The Android host verifies the signature over
sdk-plugin\tools\sign-bundle.ts:5: *   canonical_manifest_without_signature  ||  bundleHash_raw_bytes
sdk-plugin\tools\sign-bundle.ts-6- * per ``docs/crypto-spec.md`` §5. This tool computes the bundle hash,
sdk-plugin\tools\sign-bundle.ts-7- * inserts it into the manifest, computes the canonical bytes the same
sdk-plugin\tools\sign-bundle.ts:8: * way Android does, signs those, and writes the signature back into

sdk-plugin\examples\minimal\src\plugin.ts-9-// runtime `registerPlugin` call accepts placeholders because the host

40:- **Live channel.** Plugins can open a long-lived authenticated WebSocket via `platform.live.connect(channel)` for sub-second push from the sender to the device + outbound `send` from the device. The host owns the WS lifecycle (heartbeat, reconnect, rate limit, revocation-driven close); the server is a routing pipe — frames carry your V2-encrypted envelopes opaquely. **Partner wiring is not yet usable** — the device-side `LiveBridge` ships with a placeholder JWT provider that throws `no_session`; the sender-push and webhook-forwarder paths work but the device-side `platform.live.connect` will fail until the host wiring closes. See §12.
51:Two parts: a reserved **`hostPreview`** block the platform renders natively as the inbox row, and the rest of the payload that your plugin's `render()` consumes for the detail view.
135:    // is 'template' / cardType is 'live'. The validator rejects them when
144:  // call platform.showNotification; it rejects in V1 inbox mode.
149:    // not part of V1 inbox mode. Post directly to your declared endpoint.
184:- `platform.showNotification`, `platform.storage`, `platform.camera`, `platform.gallery`, `platform.file`, `platform.location`, `platform.message.respond` — *reject* with a clear error in V1 inbox mode. For V1 dogfood, stick to `network.fetch`.
198:This is the single most common manifest mistake — a plugin author declares `https://example.com/api/*` and then `fetch`-es `https://example.com/api/v1/users`, which gets rejected with `endpoint_not_declared`. The same pattern controls **template action endpoints** (the publish-time validator uses the same matcher to verify `action.endpoint` is in `endpoints`).
209:- **Timeouts**: two layers. The host's OkHttp client uses library defaults (~10s connect / 10s read each). The plugin bridge wraps every call in a hard 30s ceiling — even if the underlying HTTP request is still streaming, the bridge cancels and rejects the JS promise after 30 seconds. Plan UX around the 30s upper bound; if your endpoint is reliably slower, you need a redesign (e.g. async ack endpoint that the plugin polls).
221:    // 4xx/5xx — your backend rejected the action. Show an error in the card.
306:    manifest_hash=hashlib.sha256(b"manifest-placeholder").digest(),
307:    bundle_hash=hashlib.sha256(b"unused-for-template").digest(),
308:    bundle_signature=b"\x00" * 64,            # ignored for template
309:    signed_bundle_url="https://example.com/unused",  # ignored for template
327:**JSONPath dialect.** Only `$.field(.subfield)*` is accepted. No array indexing (`$.items[0]`), no wildcards (`$.*`), no filters (`$.items[?(@.x>0)]`). Field-name segments must match `[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*`. The server rejects manifests with malformed paths at publish time, and the client-side resolver returns `null` for malformed input as a defense-in-depth.
474:The revoke request is signed by the SDK. The `reason` value is included in the signed envelope, so an intermediary cannot strip `compromised` down to a softer classification. Re-revoking a row keeps the original `revoked_at`; a higher-severity reason can promote the row, while downgrades are ignored.
501:`ttl_seconds` defaults to 7 days. The server rejects envelopes that are already expired or more than 30 days in the future.
554:A complete `bot.py` + `plugin/` pair you can run as-is lives at `examples/trading-bot/` — `python bot.py register` → `pair` → `publish-plugin` → `ack-server` + `loop` produces a real card with a working Acknowledge round-trip. The example uses the automated V1.5 pairing flow with an in-process broker thread; `set-pairing` is preserved as a V1 manual fallback when the broker can't be reached.
667:| Broker returns 404 (pairing slot not found) | Fallback banner. Usually a multi-process miswire: the Client's `reserve()` call landed in a different process / worker than the broker's `is_reserved()` check. Use a shared `BrokerStorage` backend (Redis, Postgres) so reservations propagate. |
680:3. **Pending-pairing registry at the broker.** Before any decrypt, the broker checks `storage.is_reserved(pairing_id)` and 404s unknown IDs. An attacker who knows the public bootstrap key can mint a cryptographically valid envelope for an arbitrary uuid4, but it can't reach the decrypt path unless the Client previously reserved that ID via `create_pairing_qr(sender_broker_url=...)`.
686:- Use Redis or Postgres for `BrokerStorage`. The shipped `InMemoryBrokerStorage` is single-process only — fine for the trading-bot example where the Client and broker share one Python process, but **insufficient for multi-process deployments**. `Client.create_pairing_qr(sender_broker_url=...)` calls `storage.reserve(pairing_id)`; if the broker app runs in a different uvicorn worker, that call's effect won't be visible to the broker's `is_reserved()` check and every pairing will 404. Use a shared backing store that implements the `BrokerStorage` Protocol atomically (one `reserve` call visible to all workers).
687:- The `rate_limiter` hook on `make_app(...)` is **mandatory** in production. The pending-pairing registry blocks envelopes for un-issued `pairing_id`s, but once an attacker knows a reserved ID they can still spam decrypt attempts on it — the rate limiter is your CPU defense. Implement per-IP and per-`pairing_id` limits.
706:- **`422 manifest validation failed`** (cards/plugins publish) — Pydantic rejected the manifest body. The detail field tells you which check failed. Most common: `template required when renderer == 'template'`, `card_key_path required when card_type == 'live'`, `action endpoint not allowed by declared endpoints`. Mirror this validation client-side via `validatePluginManifest` (TS SDK) to catch before the round-trip.
714:- **`UNSIGNED-PLACEHOLDER-REPLACE-WITH-SIGN-BUNDLE` in `bundleHash` / `signature`.** You forgot to run `sign-bundle.ts` on the built bundle. The SDK validator rejects this (post Phase 4.1) — `bundleHash` must be exactly 64 hex chars (SHA-256), `signature` must be exactly 128 hex chars (Ed25519). The signer emits lowercase, but the validator and server tolerate either case. If the SDK lets it through, the server rejects at publish with 422; if both let it through, the device fails the manifest-hash check and shows "Plugin not loaded."
718:- **Template/script field mismatches.** Setting `template: {...}` on a `renderer: "script"` manifest, or omitting `template` on `renderer: "template"`. SDK validator catches both. Same with `cardType: "live"` requiring `cardKeyPath` and `cardType: "event"` rejecting it. If the SDK validator misses a case you hit, file a Phase 4.5 doc-bug.
719:- **Row shows "New message from {sender}" with fallback text only.** Your message was sent without a `hostPreview` block, or the block was malformed (logged + ignored on the device). The detail view still loads the plugin and renders normally; only the row is generic. Add a valid `hostPreview` and re-send.
755:> **V0.1 wiring caveat — partner integration must wait.** The server-side endpoints, the wire contract, and the device-side WebSocket client are all in place, but the Android plugin-host's `LiveBridge` ships with a placeholder `deviceJwtProvider` that throws `no_session` — so a plugin calling `platform.live.connect(...)` today fails with that code regardless of the rest of the wiring. The piece that's not done is connecting the host's existing device-session token provider into the bridge factory; once that lands, the surface below is usable as-is. **Read §12 to understand the contract you'll integrate against; do not write code against `platform.live.*` until the host wiring closes that gap.**
759:For TypeScript-SDK plugins today the device side is outbound-only (your plugin can `send`; inbound delivery from sender to device requires either the inbox/SSE path or — for fully-bidirectional needs — an SDK ABI bump that's not yet shipped). See the "Plugin-side" subsection below.
806:Today neither the Python nor TypeScript SDK ships a typed `live_push` helper — drive the raw POST until they do.
812:1. **Register a `liveInboundUrl`** in your plugin manifest at publish time. The TypeScript SDK manifest exposes this field; the Python `Client.publish_plugin(...)` does not yet expose `live_inbound_url`, so Python integrators must drive the raw `POST /v1/plugins/publish` body to set it until the helper grows the parameter.
839:- Verify `X-Syncler-Signature` over the **raw POST body bytes** (the compact-JSON serialization the server signed). Do not re-serialize before verifying.
880:**Inbound delivery on the TypeScript SDK is outbound-only today.** The host receives device-bound live frames and dispatches them through an `onLiveMessage` hook on the Android plugin runtime, but the TypeScript `BasePlugin` does not yet expose that hook (the `DispatchHook` union on the SDK side covers `onMessage / onAction / onDismiss / render` only). Two paths for now:
907:If you can't guarantee contiguous publishing — e.g. multiple worker processes might race on the same `(card_id, base_seq)` — fall back to `upsert_card` per tick. The server rejects gaps and replays with `409`; a non-publisher process trying to patch usually loses that race.
965:- **`native_kotlin` plugins** — signed DEX-loaded plugins running in an isolated Android process. Available to Syncler-internal first-party plugins; the public SDK + signing pipeline for partners ships later.
966:- **`script_fast`** — a Javy/QuickJS runtime alternative to the WebView for pure-logic plugins. Same wire contract as `script`; different host execution path. Not partner-ready.
968:For partner integrations today, `renderer: "script"` and `renderer: "template"` are the two options.

docs/integration-guide.md:724:Publish every plugin release as a new row under the same `plugin_identifier`. `plugin_identifier` is the stable name, while `plugin_row_id` is the exact version row returned by `client.publish_plugin(...)`.
docs/integration-guide.md:726:Version strings use semver-lite: `MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH` with an optional prerelease suffix. Each numeric component is capped at 6 digits, and every publish for the same `(sender_id, plugin_identifier)` must be strictly greater than all existing versions.

docs/integration-guide.md-348-### 5.2 Live Cards
docs/integration-guide.md-349-
docs/integration-guide.md-350-Set `card_type="live"` to ship a persistent, upsertable card. The plugin manifest must also provide `card_key_path` — a JSONPath into your decrypted payload that yields the stable identity for each card (e.g. `$.order_id`). This lets the inbox merge multiple upserts for the same logical card into one row.
docs/integration-guide.md-351-
docs/integration-guide.md-352-```python
docs/integration-guide.md-353-response = client.publish_plugin(
docs/integration-guide.md-354-    plugin_identifier="com.example.delivery",
docs/integration-guide.md-355-    version="1.0.0",
docs/integration-guide.md-356-    manifest_hash=hashlib.sha256(b"manifest").digest(),
docs/integration-guide.md-357-    bundle_hash=hashlib.sha256(open("dist/plugin.bundle.js","rb").read()).digest(),
docs/integration-guide.md-358-    bundle_signature=bytes.fromhex(manifest["signature"]),
docs/integration-guide.md-359-    signed_bundle_url="https://example.com/plugin.bundle.js",
docs/integration-guide.md-360-    capabilities=["network"],
docs/integration-guide.md-361-    endpoints=["https://example.com/api/*"],
docs/integration-guide.md-362-    card_type="live",
docs/integration-guide.md-363-    card_key_path="$.order_id",
docs/integration-guide.md-364-)
docs/integration-guide.md-365-```
docs/integration-guide.md-366-
docs/integration-guide.md-367-Live cards work with **either** renderer. The most common combo is `renderer="template"` + `card_type="live"` (delivery progress card with no JS).
docs/integration-guide.md-368-
docs/integration-guide.md-369-#### Upserting a live card
docs/integration-guide.md-370-
docs/integration-guide.md-371-```python
docs/integration-guide.md:372:from syncler.crypto import assemble_live_card_aad, encrypt_payload
docs/integration-guide.md-373-from datetime import datetime, timedelta, UTC
docs/integration-guide.md-374-import json
docs/integration-guide.md-375-
docs/integration-guide.md-376-expires_at = datetime.now(UTC) + timedelta(hours=12)
docs/integration-guide.md-377-
docs/integration-guide.md-378-# Live card plaintext. Include `hostPreview` so the inbox row uses your
docs/integration-guide.md-379-# title/subtitle instead of the generic "New message from {sender_name}"
docs/integration-guide.md-380-# fallback. The `order_id` field is what the manifest's
docs/integration-guide.md-381-# `card_key_path="$.order_id"` resolves to and MUST equal the `card_key`
docs/integration-guide.md-382-# you pass to `upsert_card` below — see the note after the example.
docs/integration-guide.md-383-plaintext = json.dumps({
docs/integration-guide.md-384-    "hostPreview": {
docs/integration-guide.md-385-        "title": "Pizza Delivery",
docs/integration-guide.md-386-        "subtitle": "Order #456",
docs/integration-guide.md-387-        "summary": "Out for delivery — ETA 8 minutes",
docs/integration-guide.md-388-    },
docs/integration-guide.md-389-    "order_id": "order-456",
docs/integration-guide.md-390-    "title": "Pizza Delivery",
docs/integration-guide.md-391-    "subtitle": "Order #456 • ETA 8 minutes",
docs/integration-guide.md-392-    "body": "Driver: Sam. Truck: 7F-12.",
docs/integration-guide.md-393-}).encode("utf-8")
docs/integration-guide.md-394-
docs/integration-guide.md-395-# AAD the device will verify against on AES-GCM decrypt. `sequence_number`
docs/integration-guide.md-396-# stays as a Python int — the canonical JSON emits an integer literal.
docs/integration-guide.md:397:aad = assemble_live_card_aad(
docs/integration-guide.md-398-    sender_id=client.sender_id,
docs/integration-guide.md-399-    user_id=user_id,
docs/integration-guide.md-400-    plugin_id=plugin_row_id,
docs/integration-guide.md-401-    card_key="order-456",
docs/integration-guide.md-402-    sequence_number=2,
docs/integration-guide.md-403-    expires_at=expires_at,
docs/integration-guide.md-404-)
docs/integration-guide.md-405-
docs/integration-guide.md-406-# V2 (current): client.upsert_card takes the plaintext payload + card_type
docs/integration-guide.md-407-# directly — the SDK handles per-device V2 envelope sealing (HPKE per
docs/integration-guide.md-408-# recipient device + AES-GCM payload, AAD bound to envelope_kind +
docs/integration-guide.md-409-# card_type + sequence_number per docs/crypto-spec.md §11). You don't
docs/integration-guide.md:410:# touch encrypt_payload / nonce yourself anymore.
docs/integration-guide.md-411-client.upsert_card(
docs/integration-guide.md-412-    user_id=user_id,
docs/integration-guide.md-413-    plugin_id=plugin_row_id,
docs/integration-guide.md-414-    card_key="order-456",
docs/integration-guide.md-415-    card_type="standard_card",
docs/integration-guide.md-416-    payload={
docs/integration-guide.md-417-        "hostPreview": {"title": "Order #456", "summary": "Shipped"},
docs/integration-guide.md-418-        "order_id": "order-456",
docs/integration-guide.md-419-        "status": "shipped",
docs/integration-guide.md-420-    },
docs/integration-guide.md-421-    sequence_number=2,
docs/integration-guide.md-422-    expires_at=expires_at,  # datetime, server caps at now + 48h
docs/integration-guide.md-423-)
docs/integration-guide.md-424-```
docs/integration-guide.md-425-
docs/integration-guide.md-426-**`card_key` must equal `card_key_path` resolved on the plaintext.** The manifest's `card_key_path` (declared at publish time, e.g. `"$.order_id"`) is the path the host extracts on decrypt to use as the merge key for this card. The `card_key` you pass to `upsert_card` is what the server stores. If the two disagree, the host's per-card merge breaks and your "live" card behaves like a series of event cards. The server doesn't cross-check the two — enforcement is your responsibility. (V1 validates `card_key_path` only as `startswith("$")`; a future phase will tighten this to the full `$.field(.subfield)*` JSONPath grammar used by template fields.)
docs/integration-guide.md-427-
docs/integration-guide.md:428:See `docs/crypto-spec.md §8` for the exact `assemble_live_card_aad` canonical byte shape.
docs/integration-guide.md-429-
docs/integration-guide.md-430-**Server-side gates on upsert** (return 4xx if violated — see §9):
docs/integration-guide.md-431-
docs/integration-guide.md-432-- **Active pairing required.** The `(sender_id, user_id)` pair must have a non-revoked `Pairing` row; otherwise 410. Mirrors the message-send path.
docs/integration-guide.md-433-- **Plugin must be active and live-type.** `Plugin.revoked_at IS NULL` and `card_type = "live"`; otherwise 410.
docs/integration-guide.md-434-- **TTL window.** `expires_at` must be strictly future (else 400) AND at most 48 hours from now (else 400). The cap is server-enforced and not negotiable.
docs/integration-guide.md-435-- **Sequence monotonicity.** New `sequence_number` must be strictly greater than the existing row's; else 409.
docs/integration-guide.md-436-- **Rate limit.** Fixed window of 60 upserts per minute per `(sender_id, user_id, card_key)`; else 429. (There's also an IP-bucketed pre-auth limit of 120/min for cheap DoS protection.)
docs/integration-guide.md-437-
docs/integration-guide.md-438-The successful response triggers a `card.upsert` SSE event to every active device of the user, so foreground clients refresh within ~1s.
docs/integration-guide.md-439-
docs/integration-guide.md-440-#### Deleting a live card
docs/integration-guide.md-441-
docs/integration-guide.md-442-```python
docs/integration-guide.md-443-client.delete_card(
docs/integration-guide.md-444-    user_id=user_id,
docs/integration-guide.md-445-    plugin_id=plugin_row_id,
docs/integration-guide.md-446-    card_key="order-456",
docs/integration-guide.md-447-)
docs/integration-guide.md-448-```
docs/integration-guide.md-449-
docs/integration-guide.md-450-`user_id` AND `plugin_id` are **required**. The canonical signed envelope binds `(sender_id, user_id, plugin_id, card_key)` — without `plugin_id` in the signature, a captured delete envelope from one of your plugins could be replayed against a different plugin row with the same `card_key`. The delete endpoint matches exactly that quadruple in the row lookup.
docs/integration-guide.md-451-
docs/integration-guide.md-452-**Freshness + replay protection:** the delete envelope also binds a `nonce` (12 random bytes) and `expires_at` (defaults to now + 24 h; server caps at 48 h). The SDK generates both automatically, so the canonical call above keeps working unchanged. Callers MAY pass `nonce=` and `expires_at=` to control the freshness window explicitly — useful when retrying after a 409 to confirm a previous delete already landed. Server-side enforcement: expired envelopes return 400; replayed nonces return 409. See `docs/crypto-spec.md §11.6` for the V2 delete envelope shape.

docs/integration-guide.md-536-
docs/integration-guide.md-537-## 8. Testing it end-to-end
docs/integration-guide.md-538-
docs/integration-guide.md-539-> **Starting a new plugin from scratch?** Run `npm create @syncler/plugin <name>` first — it scaffolds `src/plugin.ts`, `manifest.json`, `build.sh`, `package.json`, `README.md`, and `.gitignore` for you, validated against the SDK rules. Pair it with the `examples/trading-bot/` directory for a complete sender + plugin round-trip you can copy from.
docs/integration-guide.md-540-
docs/integration-guide.md-541-1. Install the Syncler Android app (`./gradlew :app:installDebug`).
docs/integration-guide.md-542-2. Sign up + log in. The app auto-enrolls this device with the server.
docs/integration-guide.md-543-3. From your backend, run a script that registers your sender and prints a pairing QR (see SDK README).
docs/integration-guide.md-544-4. Tap **Pair sender** -> **Scan QR** in the Syncler app. Confirm the fingerprint matches what your script prints.
docs/integration-guide.md-545-5. **Pairing handoff.** Two paths depending on how your sender is set up:
docs/integration-guide.md:546:   - **V1.5 automated (default going forward):** if your sender has registered a bootstrap key and supplied `sender_broker_url` at `create_pairing_qr(...)` time, the app POSTs an encrypted bootstrap envelope (containing `user_id` + `pairing_key`, sealed with the sender's X25519 bootstrap public key) to your broker after fingerprint confirmation; your sender's `Client.wait_for_pairing(...)` returns the decrypted pair. No copy step. See §8.5 for the broker setup.
docs/integration-guide.md-547-   - **V1 manual:** the app shows your `user_id` and a `pairing_key_hex`. Copy both and feed them into your backend's `client.set_pairing(user_id, pairing_key=bytes.fromhex(...))` so it can encrypt for you. This is also the fallback when the broker POST fails in the automated path.
docs/integration-guide.md-548-6. Publish your plugin (`client.publish_plugin(...)`) — save the `plugin_row_id`.
docs/integration-guide.md-549-7. Run `client.send_to(...)` with a test payload.
docs/integration-guide.md-550-8. The server pushes an `inbox.changed` event over the SSE stream the open app is subscribed to (foreground); the phone refreshes its inbox, decrypts the message, fetches your bundle (or applies your template manifest), and shows the native row within ~1s. If the app is backgrounded, FCM wakes it and the inbox refresh happens on the next foreground.
docs/integration-guide.md-551-9. Tap the row to open the plugin-rendered detail view, then tap your action button.
docs/integration-guide.md-552-10. Confirm your `/api/action` endpoint received the POST.
docs/integration-guide.md-553-
docs/integration-guide.md-554-A complete `bot.py` + `plugin/` pair you can run as-is lives at `examples/trading-bot/` — `python bot.py register` → `pair` → `publish-plugin` → `ack-server` + `loop` produces a real card with a working Acknowledge round-trip. The example uses the automated V1.5 pairing flow with an in-process broker thread; `set-pairing` is preserved as a V1 manual fallback when the broker can't be reached.
docs/integration-guide.md-555-
docs/integration-guide.md-556-## 8.5 Automated pairing (V1.5)
docs/integration-guide.md-557-
docs/integration-guide.md-558-V1 pairing makes the user copy `user_id` + `pairing_key_hex` by hand into your sender's CLI. V1.5 replaces step 5 above with an encrypted POST: after the user confirms the fingerprint, the app silently delivers the pairing key to your sender's broker. The user never sees a hex blob.
docs/integration-guide.md-559-
docs/integration-guide.md-560-The protocol underneath (HPKE-style envelope, X25519 ECDH, AES-GCM with AAD-binding) is in `docs/crypto-spec.md §9`. This section is the integration walkthrough.
docs/integration-guide.md-561-
docs/integration-guide.md-562-### Why automated
docs/integration-guide.md-563-
docs/integration-guide.md-564-- One-step pairing for users.
docs/integration-guide.md-565-- Production senders don't need a manual-paste UX.
docs/integration-guide.md:566:- The substitution-attack guards (Ed25519 over the bootstrap key + AAD-binding on `sender_broker_url`) keep V1's trust model intact.
docs/integration-guide.md-567-
docs/integration-guide.md-568-### Sender setup
docs/integration-guide.md-569-
docs/integration-guide.md-570-You need to do four things, once:
docs/integration-guide.md-571-
docs/integration-guide.md:572:1. **Generate an X25519 bootstrap keypair** alongside your existing Ed25519 signing key. Persist the private key somewhere safe (file with 0600, KMS, secrets manager).
docs/integration-guide.md-573-
docs/integration-guide.md-574-   ```python
docs/integration-guide.md:575:   from syncler.bootstrap import x25519_keypair_pem
docs/integration-guide.md:576:   bootstrap_priv, bootstrap_pub_raw = x25519_keypair_pem()
docs/integration-guide.md:577:   # bootstrap_pub_raw is 32 raw bytes — store both fields somewhere
docs/integration-guide.md-578-   # your broker process can read them.
docs/integration-guide.md-579-   ```
docs/integration-guide.md-580-
docs/integration-guide.md:581:2. **Register the bootstrap key with syncler** (once per sender, or per rotation):
docs/integration-guide.md-582-
docs/integration-guide.md-583-   ```python
docs/integration-guide.md:584:   client.register_bootstrap_key(bootstrap_public_key_raw=bootstrap_pub_raw)
docs/integration-guide.md-585-   ```
docs/integration-guide.md-586-
docs/integration-guide.md:587:   Internally the SDK Ed25519-signs `b"syncler-v1-bootstrap-key:" + bootstrap_pub_raw` and POSTs to `/v1/senders/me/bootstrap-key`.
docs/integration-guide.md-588-
docs/integration-guide.md-589-3. **Run a broker** that your devices POST to. The SDK ships one under the `[broker]` extra:
docs/integration-guide.md-590-
docs/integration-guide.md-591-   ```sh
docs/integration-guide.md-592-   pip install 'syncler[broker]'
docs/integration-guide.md-593-   ```
docs/integration-guide.md-594-
docs/integration-guide.md-595-   ```python
docs/integration-guide.md-596-   # broker_app.py
docs/integration-guide.md-597-   from syncler.broker import make_app
docs/integration-guide.md-598-   from syncler.broker_storage import InMemoryBrokerStorage
docs/integration-guide.md:599:   from syncler.bootstrap import load_x25519_private_key_from_raw
docs/integration-guide.md-600-
docs/integration-guide.md-601-   storage = InMemoryBrokerStorage()
docs/integration-guide.md:602:   priv = load_x25519_private_key_from_raw(open("bootstrap.key", "rb").read())
docs/integration-guide.md:603:   pub_raw = open("bootstrap.pub", "rb").read()
docs/integration-guide.md-604-
docs/integration-guide.md-605-   app = make_app(
docs/integration-guide.md:606:       bootstrap_private_key=priv,
docs/integration-guide.md:607:       bootstrap_public_key_raw=pub_raw,
docs/integration-guide.md:608:       sender_broker_url="https://sender.example.com/syncler/bootstrap",
docs/integration-guide.md-609-       storage=storage,
docs/integration-guide.md-610-   )
docs/integration-guide.md-611-   ```
docs/integration-guide.md-612-
docs/integration-guide.md-613-   ```sh
docs/integration-guide.md-614-   uvicorn broker_app:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8443 \
docs/integration-guide.md-615-       --ssl-keyfile=key.pem --ssl-certfile=cert.pem
docs/integration-guide.md-616-   ```
docs/integration-guide.md-617-
docs/integration-guide.md:618:   Your reverse proxy maps `https://sender.example.com/syncler/bootstrap` → this app.
docs/integration-guide.md-619-
docs/integration-guide.md-620-4. **Tell `Client` where the broker storage lives** so `wait_for_pairing` can poll it:
docs/integration-guide.md-621-
docs/integration-guide.md-622-   ```python
docs/integration-guide.md-623-   client = Client(
docs/integration-guide.md-624-       sender_name="Example",
docs/integration-guide.md-625-       private_key_path="sender.pem",
docs/integration-guide.md-626-       broker_storage=storage,            # NEW — same storage the broker app writes to
docs/integration-guide.md-627-   )
docs/integration-guide.md:628:   # The sender's X25519 bootstrap keypair lives outside Client (the
docs/integration-guide.md-629-   # broker app holds it). The Client only needs the storage handle
docs/integration-guide.md-630-   # to learn when a pairing has completed.
docs/integration-guide.md-631-
docs/integration-guide.md-632-   path = client.create_pairing_qr(
docs/integration-guide.md-633-       ttl_seconds=300,
docs/integration-guide.md-634-       out_path="pair.png",
docs/integration-guide.md:635:       sender_broker_url="https://sender.example.com/syncler/bootstrap",  # NEW
docs/integration-guide.md-636-   )
docs/integration-guide.md-637-
docs/integration-guide.md-638-   # Blocks until the broker writes (user_id, pairing_key) to storage.
docs/integration-guide.md-639-   # On success the Pairing dataclass carries `pairing_id` + `user_id`;
docs/integration-guide.md-640-   # the 32-byte pairing key is stored on `client.pairing_key` so
docs/integration-guide.md-641-   # subsequent `send_to(...)` calls just work.
docs/integration-guide.md-642-   pairing = client.wait_for_pairing(timeout_seconds=120)
docs/integration-guide.md-643-   # `client.pairing_key` is now set; no further `set_pairing` call needed.
docs/integration-guide.md-644-   ```
docs/integration-guide.md-645-
docs/integration-guide.md-646-### Android user flow
docs/integration-guide.md-647-
docs/integration-guide.md:648:When the device fetches the preview and sees all four bootstrap fields, the pairing screen uses the automated path:
docs/integration-guide.md-649-
docs/integration-guide.md-650-1. User scans the QR.
docs/integration-guide.md:651:2. App fetches preview; verifies `bootstrap_key_signature` against the sender's Ed25519 pub key.
docs/integration-guide.md-652-3. User confirms the fingerprint (unchanged).
docs/integration-guide.md-653-4. App calls `/complete` — Syncler-side pairing is finalized.
docs/integration-guide.md:654:5. App builds the bootstrap envelope and POSTs to `sender_broker_url`.
docs/integration-guide.md-655-6. Broker decrypts, writes to its storage, returns 201.
docs/integration-guide.md-656-7. `Client.wait_for_pairing` in your sender's send loop picks up the new pairing tuple.
docs/integration-guide.md-657-
docs/integration-guide.md-658-If step 5 fails (broker down, network glitch), the app **shows a fallback banner** with the same `user_id` + `pairing_key_hex` block as the V1 manual flow. The device-side pairing is already real (step 4 finalized it); only the sender catch-up needs the manual paste.
docs/integration-guide.md-659-
docs/integration-guide.md-660-### Failure modes
docs/integration-guide.md-661-
docs/integration-guide.md-662-| What | App behavior |
docs/integration-guide.md-663-|---|---|
docs/integration-guide.md:664:| `bootstrap_key_signature` doesn't verify | **Hard refusal.** Pairing NOT finalized. Treated as substitution-attack indicator — user does not get a fallback. |
docs/integration-guide.md:665:| Preview has only some bootstrap fields | **Hard refusal**, same reason. Sender MUST register all four atomically. |
docs/integration-guide.md-666-| Broker returns 401 (decrypt failed) | Fallback banner (manual paste). |
docs/integration-guide.md-667-| Broker returns 404 (pairing slot not found) | Fallback banner. Usually a multi-process miswire: the Client's `reserve()` call landed in a different process / worker than the broker's `is_reserved()` check. Use a shared `BrokerStorage` backend (Redis, Postgres) so reservations propagate. |
docs/integration-guide.md-668-| Broker returns 409 (replay with different values) | Fallback banner — the pairing key from your sender's `wait_for_pairing` will not match this device's, so the user must paste the device's values. |
docs/integration-guide.md-669-| Broker returns 5xx or times out | Retried up to 3 attempts (250ms / 750ms backoff). If still failing, fallback banner. |
docs/integration-guide.md-670-| Sender's `wait_for_pairing` times out | Sender's responsibility — typically loop again with a fresh QR. The device-side pairing is still real; user can paste manually. |
docs/integration-guide.md-671-
docs/integration-guide.md-672-### Security boundaries
docs/integration-guide.md-673-
docs/integration-guide.md-674-Four guards make this safe:
docs/integration-guide.md-675-
docs/integration-guide.md:676:1. **Bootstrap key signature.** The sender's X25519 bootstrap public key is signed by its long-term Ed25519 signing key (which the user confirms via fingerprint). The Syncler server cannot substitute its own X25519 key — the signature would fail to verify against the Ed25519 pub key the user just confirmed.
docs/integration-guide.md-677-
docs/integration-guide.md-678-2. **AAD-binding on `sender_broker_url`.** Even if the Syncler server could change which URL the device POSTs to, the broker reconstructs AAD using its OWN configured `sender_broker_url`. An envelope crafted for a different URL fails AEAD tag verification.
docs/integration-guide.md-679-
docs/integration-guide.md:680:3. **Pending-pairing registry at the broker.** Before any decrypt, the broker checks `storage.is_reserved(pairing_id)` and 404s unknown IDs. An attacker who knows the public bootstrap key can mint a cryptographically valid envelope for an arbitrary uuid4, but it can't reach the decrypt path unless the Client previously reserved that ID via `create_pairing_qr(sender_broker_url=...)`.
docs/integration-guide.md-681-
docs/integration-guide.md-682-4. **CAS replay guard at the broker.** A captured envelope replayed to the same broker with the same plaintext is idempotent (200). Replayed with different values gets a 409.
docs/integration-guide.md-683-
docs/integration-guide.md-684-### Production hardening
docs/integration-guide.md-685-
docs/integration-guide.md-686-- Use Redis or Postgres for `BrokerStorage`. The shipped `InMemoryBrokerStorage` is single-process only — fine for the trading-bot example where the Client and broker share one Python process, but **insufficient for multi-process deployments**. `Client.create_pairing_qr(sender_broker_url=...)` calls `storage.reserve(pairing_id)`; if the broker app runs in a different uvicorn worker, that call's effect won't be visible to the broker's `is_reserved()` check and every pairing will 404. Use a shared backing store that implements the `BrokerStorage` Protocol atomically (one `reserve` call visible to all workers).
docs/integration-guide.md-687-- The `rate_limiter` hook on `make_app(...)` is **mandatory** in production. The pending-pairing registry blocks envelopes for un-issued `pairing_id`s, but once an attacker knows a reserved ID they can still spam decrypt attempts on it — the rate limiter is your CPU defense. Implement per-IP and per-`pairing_id` limits.
docs/integration-guide.md-688-- Terminate TLS at your reverse proxy; the broker is HTTP-only inside the trust boundary.
docs/integration-guide.md-689-- See `docs/crypto-spec.md §9.3` for the single-fixed-`sender_broker_url` design (per-pairing URLs are a V2 add).
docs/integration-guide.md-690-
docs/integration-guide.md-691-## 9. Common server errors
docs/integration-guide.md-692-
docs/integration-guide.md-693-The server returned a 4xx or 5xx; here's what to change.
docs/integration-guide.md-694-
docs/integration-guide.md-695-- **`401 invalid envelope signature`** — your sender's Ed25519 key doesn't match the one registered. Re-check `private_key_path`.
docs/integration-guide.md-696-- **`410 plugin missing, revoked, or not owned by sender`** — `plugin_id` (the row UUID) is wrong, or you accidentally used `plugin_identifier`. The two are distinct (see §5 vs §6).
docs/integration-guide.md-697-- **`410 recipient has no active devices`** — the user has no enrolled device (signed up but never opened the app, or revoked all devices). FCM availability is *not* required; messages are stored regardless and pulled via `/v1/messages/inbox`.
docs/integration-guide.md-698-- **`409 nonce already used`** — your code reused a nonce somehow; SDK generates fresh nonces per `send_to`, so this means you're calling `send_to` with cached envelope bytes. Rebuild.
--

sdk-python/syncler/client.py-245-
sdk-python/syncler/client.py-246-    def wait_for_pairing(
sdk-python/syncler/client.py-247-        self,
sdk-python/syncler/client.py-248-        *,
sdk-python/syncler/client.py-249-        timeout_seconds: int = 120,
sdk-python/syncler/client.py-250-        poll_interval_seconds: float = 1.0,
sdk-python/syncler/client.py-251-    ) -> Pairing:
sdk-python/syncler/client.py-252-        """V1.5 Phase 5a-2: poll the broker storage until the device's
sdk-python/syncler/client.py:253:        bootstrap POST lands, then automatically set the pairing on
sdk-python/syncler/client.py-254-        this client.
sdk-python/syncler/client.py-255-
